subject: Chemistry
title: Azo Dyes: Most Colors You Wear
description: A 60-second lesson on Azo Dyes: Most Colors You Wear.

hook.text: Over 60% of all dyes used today are azo dyes—a single chemical family colors most of the world's textiles.

buildup.text: Azo dyes contain the -N=N- group, a double bond between two nitrogen atoms that creates a rigid, colored molecule.

discovery.text: By changing the groups attached to the N=N bond, chemists create reds, oranges, yellows, and blues on demand.

twist.text: Some azo dyes break down into carcinogenic amines. The EU banned 22 of them in textiles—but they're still used elsewhere.

climax.text: Despite risks, azo dyes are cheap, bright, and versatile. Over 3,000 different azo dyes exist in industrial use today.

punchline.text: Two nitrogens with a double bond. One chemical trick colors the modern world.

quiz.question: What makes azo dyes so versatile?
options: Changing groups around the -N=N- bond produces different colors on demand
They can only be applied to synthetic fabrics
They change color depending on temperature
